course_description: Flow of funds & financial markets, theory & behaviour of interest rates, term & risk structure of interest rates, exchange rates, interest parity, expectations formation, equity markets, debt market, Euro markets, derivatives markets, social allocation of capital.

requirements.assumed_background: The course ECON3210 Financial Markets and Institutions is intended for students who contemplate a deep and quite technical understanding of financial markets and institutions. It is essential therefore that students who take this course have been exposed to financial topics in previous courses. Please check the pre-requisites listed above, which should be completed before enrolling in this course. It is not recommended to take the pre-requisite courses during the same semester as ECON3210.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
The workshop will commence in Week 1. We will use the first session to complete topic 1 on Financial Intermediation. From week 2 the workshop will be devoted to training exercises on the topic of the previous week.

Book into your diary NOW the following possible dates for the mid-semester exam: Saturday 31st August, 7th, or 14th September 2013 . The mid-semester exam is managed centrally by the University. The exact date will be announced by the University in mid-August. Please keep all these dates free of any other commitments for the time being.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: This course aims to provide students with a thorough understanding of the current issues and debates concerning financial markets and institutions. The course has a focus on applied issues and encourages students to refer to many sources of information such as websites of financial institutions and financial publications so that students can acquire a greater hands-on knowledge of the real world of finance. The course aims further to develop students analytical and technical skills and to encourage lateral thinking.

learning_outcomes.description: identify why financial markets and financial intermediaries are crucial to a well functioning economy.
understand how the financial system in the U.S. is both similar to and different from financial systems in other countries.
represent financial intermediaries operations through their T-accounts.
realize to what extent asymmetric information explains the way our financial system is structured.
distinguish the different types of conflicts of interest in the financial industry and discuss policies to remedy them.
calculate and interpret some banks performance indicators.
show how banks statements are affected by defaulting loans and devalued assets.
describe the process of securitization by lenders.
understand the benefits and dangers associated with securitisation.
apply the basics of interest gap analysis.
describe the operations that could increase or decrease the interest rate risk exposure of a bank.
describe the recent changes in international regulation on bank capital adequacy
understand the effect of the new capital adequacy regulation on the risk taking behaviour of banks.
describe how central banks contribute to the stability of the financial markets.
understand why and how most central banks currently set a target for the overnight interest rate.
describe how central banks have altered their tools for implementing monetary policy during the recent financial crisis.
understand the practice and implications of short-selling.
describe the changes in the regulation of short-selling.
understand the conflicts of interest in the mutual funds industry.
understand how late and timing trade can lead to fraud.
distinguish between mutual funds and hedge funds.
describe the various causes of the subprime mortgage crisis.
understand how a financial crisis can spread across countries and sectors.
understand factors contributing to default on sovereign debt.
analyze the contagion effects of a sovereign debt default.

assessment_details.format: Multiple-choice, Short answer, Short essay, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The mid-semester exam will take place outside the lectures and workshop slots, on Saturday 31st August , 7th or 14th September 2013 , and will be administered by the University. The exact date will be communicated to the School around mid-August 2013, and students are urged to keep the three dates free of any other commitments for the time being. Students who are working during weekends should already inform their employer about these three dates, as work commitments will not be considered as a valid extenuating circumstance for not attending the exam . The exam will last 90 minutes plus 10 minutes perusal.

The topics included in the exam will depend on the actual date for the mid-semester exam. The contents of the exam will be topics 1-5.

assessment_details.title: Training
assessment_details.task_description:
This test comprises 20-25 questions and will last 60 minutes.

This test does not count towards the final mark but is compulsory. The test has been designed to give a chance to students to become familiar with the technology involved in online tests and to get a feeling of the style of the questions they could expect. Students should undertake this training test in the same conditions as the ones that will prevail for the actual tests, i.e. with the same computer, the same Internet connection and in the same room.

IMPORTANT NOTICE Students who do not undertake this compulsory training test will not be entitled to extenuating circumstances related to technical problems that may occur during the later tests that are part of the formal assessment.

For each topic, students will be provided with a list of required and recommended readings. All of them will be available directly from Blackboard. The reading list for a given topic will be clearly indicated in the first slides of each topic in order to include in it the most relevant articles available at that time.

You will be able to access the lecture slides and the associated resources via "Blackboard" a week in advance.

All lectures are recorded with Echo360 and the recording is available on Blackboard the week after the lecture.

ECON3210 does not include tutorials but a workshop that the lecturer devotes to training exercises. The lecturer provides handouts with questions (and very detailed model answers at the end of the same week) to provide the students with an opportunity to train and self-assess. Quizzes for each topic are available on Blackboard for training purposes. Each online test will be preceded by a training test the week before.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University’s assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

Feedback in this Course

Apart from the formal BEL couse evaluation at the end of the course, you are encouraged to express your concerns or suggestions at any time. We shall devote the first 5 minutes of each lecture for such informal feedback.

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

Definition of Plagiarism

The act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another either intentionally or unintentionally. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Examples of plagiarism are;

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence;

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence with an end reference but without quotation marks around the copied text;

Copying ideas, concepts, research results, computer codes, statistical tables, designs, images, sounds or text or any combination of these;

Paraphrasing, summarising or simply rearranging another person's words, ideas, etc without changing the basic structure and/or meaning of the text;

Offering an idea or interpretation that is not one's own without identifying whose idea or interpretation it is;

A ‘cut and paste' of statements from multiple sources;

Presenting as independent, work done in collaboration with others;

Copying or adapting another student's original work into a submitted assessment item.

Importance of Referencing

Complete and accurate referencing is essential for academic writing. There are three main reasons why full referencing is essential.

1. The first is that when another writer’s work is used without being referenced the act of plagiarism has been committed. Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one’s own work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another and is covered by the University's Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy. The penalty for plagiarism under the Policy can range from zero marks for the assessment item to cancellation of credit for the course.

2. The second reason for referencing is so that anyone reading the paper can follow up any ideas or concepts that have been presented. This is especially important in academic writing where people involved in research may read the paper. One of the main ways of researching a topic is to read what other people have written and then follow up some of the references they have cited. Then, after those ‘follow-up’ papers have been read the researcher can continue the search-trail by tracking down more references cited in those follow-up papers, and so on. This is known as a bibliographical chain. This research is difficult if not impossible to complete if papers are not fully and accurately referenced.

3. Thirdly, referencing is important as it adds credibility to the argument that you are presenting. If an argument is to have any credibility it must be supported by evidence and that evidence must be referenced.

If a reader of your paper can read something that you have written and ask questions like; who says? Or How do you know that? Or On what do you base that comment? Then what you have written needs to be referenced. For more information on the importance of, and direction on referencing please refer to the School of Economics Assignment Presentation Guide .

Consequences of Plagiarism

Students should be aware that the School of Economics does not hesitate to use the procedures available under the University of Queensland Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy to investigate suspected misconduct involving plagiarism.

The penalties imposed for cases where a finding of misconduct is made vary, based on the seriousness of the case and range across but are not limited to:

a reduction in the mark for the assessment piece or cancellation of the mark for the assessment piece;

a requirement to complete further or repeat work for the subject;

the cancellation of credit for the subject;

expulsion from the University.

Students should also be aware of the fact that when a finding of misconduct is made that this is recorded on the University’s central misconduct database.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Other Course Guidelines

A significant part of the formal assessment for this course (20%) takes the form of online tests run at home. While students are allowed to use their textbooks and lecture notes, resorting to the help of a third party is strictly forbidden when doing the online tests.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
ECON3210 Financial Markets and Institutions is in the core course list for the Bachelor of Economics Major International Trade and Finance and in the Bachelor of Commerce Major Finance (starting in 2012). Note that the students in the Bachelor of Commerce who started before 2012 have still the opportunity to choose ECON2200 Management of Financial Institutions instead. ECON3210 is an elective course for all Majors in the Bachelor of Economics.

The course aims to provide a deep and thorough understanding of the financial markets and institutions. The main aim of this course is to present material that is of great relevance for the understanding of the current financial environment. For that purpose, all topics selected this year will have made (and likely will still do) the headlines of the financial newspapers in the last twelve months. We will cover in detail, among other topics, the subprime-mortgage crisis, the European sovereign debt crisis, Fed quantitative easing and the debates on reforming regulation in financial markets in general and bank capital adequacy regulation in particular.

The topics covered this year will be the following:

Financial Intermediation

An Economic Analysis of Financial Structure

Mark-to-market accounting and default crisis in banks financial statements

Securitisation

Bank Interest-rate risk Management

Reforms of Basel capital adequacy regulation

New Implementation of Monetary Policy

Short-Selling

The Challenges of the Managed Funds Industry

The subprime mortgage crisis and its consequences

The Sovereign debt crisis

The level of technicality and the topics targeted require that some general knowledge on financial institutions and markets be acquired beforehand. It is essential that the pre-requisites requirements are met before the course starts. In particular it is not recommended to take ECON2200 during the same semester as ECON3210. No traditional textbook currently available on the market would give the appropriate coverage required for this course. The course therefore mainly relies on recent articles published by financial newspapers, central banks, financial regulators, financial markets operators and international organisations like IMF, OECD or BIS. ALL reading resources are made available through Blackboard. In the absence of a single textbook, the slides for the course are much more elaborate and detailed than usual and have to be considered as study handouts compiling the main ideas of the reading.

Lectures and workshop are recorded with Echo360 (formerly called Lectopia), and the files are released the day after the actual delivery of the content. Each topic covered in the lectures is the subject of a workshop the week after, followed by training exercises and self-assessed quizzes. By the end of the week the model answers to the workshop questions are provided on Blackboard. The course makes an intensive use of Blackboard, with the upload of many resources (slides, workshop and training handouts, model answers, extra reading), the availability of quizzes online as well as a vibrant discussion board organised by topic and supervised by the tutor and the lecturer. This year for the second time we will use a virtual classroom on Blackboard, in which the lecturer will provide online consultation in addition to the usual face-to-face sessions.

Dr Frédérique Bracoud, the course coordinator and lecturer for the seventh consecutive year, is a theoretical banking Microeconomist, trained in France and Belgium, and has a record of 13 year-experience in teaching Financial Economics at introductory and advanced levels in the UK and in Australia. She implemented major changes 4 years ago and is now confident that the course has the right structure. As a result, no major adjustments will be made this year, and one can expect a smooth operation in spite of the large audience involved (the enrolment was 284 students last year).

As students like to know how the previous cohort performed in this course the year before, the grade distribution for 2012 is provided in the table below. The data account for the special and supplementary exams results and exclude the students who have withdrawn from the course. This is a course where very good students can secure the highest grade as illustrated by the large proportion of 7 (more than 14%) in the table below. It should be flagged that unfortunately a significant proportion of students (around 12%) also failed the course last year.

Please be aware that the course applies a slightly different grading system from the one in other courses. In addition to the traditional criterion that applies on the average mark across assessments, the course grading system also imposes some requirements on the performance in some particular pieces of assessment: either the mid-semester exam OR the final exam must be in the range of the grade too. For more details please refer to the assessment part of the course profile and to the handout entitled "The Grading System in ECON3210" available on Blackboard under the heading "Assessment". A grade simulator can be found at the same location.

Since it has been redesigned in 2009, the course ECON3210 Financial Markets and Institutions has been well received by students. Last year the course was rated 4.55/5 and the lecturer 4.75/5, which is an outstanding result. Students who liked the course over the years often mention 1) the relevance of the material and how up to date the information provided was 2) the lecturer’s enthusiasm and knowledge of the subject and 3) the diversity and quantity of learning materials available through Blackboard. The weekly one hour workshop meets the needs for training in the absence of tutorials. This year for the first time a tutor will join the team to assist the lecturer to provide support to students.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Among the positive aspects of the course, students reported in their evaluation that they appreciated the course's interesting topics, their link with the real world, the approachable, cheerful, and inspiring nature of the lecturer, the detailed model answers of the tutorial questions, the continuous assessment with the online quizzes preceded by training quizzes, the feedback incorporated in online tests, the interesting reading, the lecturer's handouts to complement the readings and lectures' recordings. Many students also praised the intensive use of Blackboard.

Few aspects were a source of frustration for some students last year. Please find a summary of the issues some students had last year and a description of the way Frédérique intends to address them during this academic year.

1) Too many e-mails

Students used to receive an e-mail each time resources were uploaded on Blackboard. Many students loved it but some students complained that important e-mails were lost because of the quantity of e-mails. It has been decided this year that only important information put on Blackboard will be accompanied by e-mails.

2) Resources not put early enough on Blackboard

Last year students had access to the slides two days before the lecture and to tutorial questions on the Friday of the week before. An effort will be made to provide the slides and the tutorial questions one week in advance.

3) "Too" demanding grading system

This course applies an extra requirement when assigning the grade at the end of the exam period. In order to reach a grade (say grade 4) not only the average must be above a certain threshold (50%) but also one of the main components - either the mid-term OR the final exam - must be above that threshold (50%). The new grading rules will be applied this year as well. Please refer to the handout on Blackboard entitled "Grading system in ECON3210" located under Assessment for more information on that matter.

A better understanding of the rationales behind such a system may help relieve some of the anxiety experienced last year. The new grading rules are a response to the intensive use of formative online tests that are done at home and can therefore benefit from the help of others. The new grading system aims at preventing some rare but inacceptable cases, such as the following one: a student gets an average of 50 overall resulting from 40 at the mid-term exam, 40 at the final exam, and 90 at the two online tests, possibly thanks to the help of clever and devoted friends. When on his/her own, during the class test or the final exam, the student clearly does not manage to reach 50% at all and grade 4 based exclusively on the average would not reflect the student's real ability. With the current course grading system, the student would miss a 4 in spite of the 50 average and gets only a 3, which - we all agree - is a fairer outcome.

The new system turned out to be a good stimulant for studying in a consistent manner throughout the semester: at the end of the assessment period no student was kept at a lower grade than otherwise due to the implementation of the new grading rules. The system worked well as the students actually adjusted their study to account for it and surely a lot of extra learning took place thanks to its implementation.

The system recognises that a student cannot always perform at his/her best (one accident is acceptable) and that is the reason why the extra requirement applies to only ONE main piece of assessment, not to both. The grading system is simply finding evidence of the ability of the student to reach a certain standard on his/her own and is not checking whether he/she is completely consistent across all his/her pieces of assessment.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
The lectures will be supported by slides, elaborating topics from the main readings and providing background information as well as additional explanations. Attendance is not a formal requirement, but highly recommended. Echo360 recording is available the day after the lecture. Be aware of the dangers of not attending lectures and relying exclusively on Echo360: firstly one cannot be entirely sure that there will not be any technical problem making the recording inoperational and secondly the students may postpone the listening too much andand become overwhelmed. Students using Echo360 recording as a substitute to lecture attendance should have the discipline to listen the recording by the end of the same week.

Workshops reinforce the material seen at the lectures and take place the week after the lecture. Detailed model answers are available on the week end that follows the workshop. Echo360 is also available for the workshop the day after the lecture.

If students have difficulties relating to the contents of the course and did not manage to have an answer during lectures and workshops, they have access to many other venues to raise their questions. The Blackoard discussion board is the first place where questions easy to formulate should be directed. The discussion board is organised through forum, one for each topic of the course. Both the tutor and lecturer are committed to reply any enquiries on Blackboard within 48 hours in week days. Students can also help each other on Blackboard and a gift (movie ticket) will go to the student who has helped the others in the most efficient way. If the question is hard to write then the consultation with our tutor is the right venue to raise the question. If the question is hard and the tutor rightly points out that they do not know the answer, then a consultation with the lecturer is possible. Finally for out-of-office hours, the course will have an online consultation via Blackboard Virtual classroom with lecturer/tutor during a week day (TBA) from 8:00pm to 9:00pm to suit the needs of working students.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

No understanding of lecture material, and assigned reading.

A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 0.00-19.99%.

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Deficient understanding of lecture material, and assigned reading.

A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 20.00-44.99%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Superficial or partial or faulty understanding of lecture material, and assigned reading.

Option 1 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 45.00-49.99%

Option 2 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 50.00-64.99% AND the performance in the mid-term exam and the final exam below 50.00%.

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Adequate understanding and correct statement of facts and propositions, does not cover all major details and gives analyses, syntheses and critique containing some major deficiencies. Makes some errors but shows evidence of a basic knowledge of lecture material, and assigned reading.

Option 1 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 50.00-64.99% AND the performance in either the mid-term exam or the final exam equal or above 50.00%.

Option 2 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 65.00-74.99% AND the performance in the mid-term exam and the final exam below 65.00%.

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Sound statement of facts and propositions and gives analyses, syntheses and critique containing no major deficiencies. Makes no significant errors and shows evidence of a sound to good knowledge of lecture material, and assigned reading.

Option 1 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 65.00-74.99% AND the performance in either the mid-term exam or the final exam equal or above 65.00%.

Option 2 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 75.00-84.99% AND the performance in the mid-term exam and the final exam below 75.00%.

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

Provides an accurate and complete statement and gives clear and consistent analyses, syntheses and critique. Makes no significant errors and shows evidence of a sound to good knowledge of lecture material, and assigned reading.

Option 1 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 75.00-84.99% AND the performance in either the mid-term exam or the final exam equal or above 75.00%.

Option 2 A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 85.00-100% AND the performance in the mid-term exam and the final exam below 85.00%.

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Provides accurate and complete statements and gives clear and consistent analyses, syntheses and critique. Makes no errors and shows evidence of a complete knowledge of lecture material, and assigned reading.

A weighted average performance over all assessments in the range 85.00-100% AND the performance in either the mid-term exam or the final exam equal or above 85.00%.

Other Requirements & Comments:

The "weighted average performance over all assessments" used in the definitions above is calculated with the weight of 10% for each online test, 30% for the mid-term exam and 50% for the final exam as stipulated in the summary at the top of the page.

A detailed description of the grading system with many examples and with the rationales for using these grading criteria is provided in the lecturer's handout "The grading system in ECON3210" available on Blackboard under Assessment. A grade simulator is available at the same location.
late_submission: The online tests are not available on Blackboard after the due date and therefore a late submission is irrelevant for the online tests. If a student fails to do the online test by the due date, the mark 0 is allocated to the piece of assessment. If a student has a medical certificate covering the whole period of the availability of the test online or any other valid reason (as defined by the University regulation) for not taking the test, he/she should inform the lecturer as early as possible before the due date and a specific arrangement will be considered on a case-by-case basis.
other_assessment_information:
The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available under Section 6. Policies and Guidelines - 6.1. Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines of this Course Profile.

DEFERRED EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to attend a scheduled exam (e.g. due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to sit a deferred examination at a later time.

Information about deferred examinations can be found on the web at http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2995 and also on the School of Economics website

at http://www.uq.edu.au/economics/policy-on-absences-from-mid-semester-examinations Please read BOTH these websites very carefully.

All applications for deferred exams must be submitted within (5) calendar days of the original date of the examination.

Mid-semester exam (SCHOOL exam ) - submit your application to the Course Administrator Christina Ellsworth with appropriate documentation.

End of Semester Examination (CENTRAL exam) - submit your application to the Student Centre, Level 1, J.D. Story Building with appropriate documentation.

SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATIONS

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you can find information about supplementary examinations at: http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2954 . Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for a supplementary examination. Also see General Award Rules, Section 1A.17.3, and your program rules.

Applications for supplementary exams must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 days of the official release of results.

Important Information regarding Supplementary Examinations for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

· Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

· Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

· Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

· Students are advised to take the Deferred & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans (11-13 December).
